examples.explanation: Start: helper(2,7). i=2, weight[2]=5 ≤7, so max(helper(1,7), 60+helper(1,2)). Compute helper(1,7): i=1, weight[1]=4 ≤7, so max(helper(0,7), 50+helper(0,3)). Compute helper(0,7): i=0, weight[0]=3 ≤7, so max(helper(-1,7), 30+helper(-1,4)). helper(-1,7)=0 (base case). helper(-1,4)=0. So helper(0,7)=max(0,30+0)=30. Now compute helper(0,3): i=0, weight[0]=3 ≤3, so max(helper(-1,3), 30+helper(-1,0)). helper(-1,3)=0, helper(-1,0)=0, so helper(0,3)=max(0,30)=30. Back to helper(1,7): max(30, 50+30)=max(30,80)=80. Now compute helper(1,2): i=1, weight[1]=4 >2, so return helper(0,2). helper(0,2): i=0, weight[0]=3 >2, so return helper(-1,2)=0. Back to helper(2,7): max(80, 60+0)=max(80,60)=80. Final output: 80. Wait, correction: Let's recompute carefully. Actually, with weights [3,4,5] and values [30,50,60], capacity 7: Best is items 0 and 1 (weight 3+4=7, value 30+50=80). So output should be 80. My trace gave 80, but I wrote 90 earlier—correcting. Output is 80.

Start: helper(3,10). i=3, weight[3]=5 ≤10, so max(helper(2,10), 6+helper(2,5)). Compute helper(2,10): i=2, weight[2]=4 ≤10, so max(helper(1,10), 5+helper(1,6)). Compute helper(1,10): i=1, weight[1]=3 ≤10, so max(helper(0,10), 4+helper(0,7)). Compute helper(0,10): i=0, weight[0]=2 ≤10, so max(helper(-1,10), 3+helper(-1,8)). helper(-1,10)=0, helper(-1,8)=0, so helper(0,10)=max(0,3)=3. Compute helper(0,7): i=0, weight[0]=2 ≤7, so max(helper(-1,7), 3+helper(-1,5)). helper(-1,7)=0, helper(-1,5)=0, so helper(0,7)=max(0,3)=3. So helper(1,10)=max(3, 4+3)=max(3,7)=7. Compute helper(1,6): i=1, weight[1]=3 ≤6, so max(helper(0,6), 4+helper(0,3)). helper(0,6): i=0, weight[0]=2 ≤6, so max(helper(-1,6), 3+helper(-1,4)). helper(-1,6)=0, helper(-1,4)=0, so helper(0,6)=max(0,3)=3. helper(0,3): i=0, weight[0]=2 ≤3, so max(helper(-1,3), 3+helper(-1,1)). helper(-1,3)=0, helper(-1,1)=0, so helper(0,3)=max(0,3)=3. So helper(1,6)=max(3, 4+3)=max(3,7)=7. So helper(2,10)=max(7, 5+7)=max(7,12)=12. Now compute helper(2,5): i=2, weight[2]=4 ≤5, so max(helper(1,5), 5+helper(1,1)). helper(1,5): i=1, weight[1]=3 ≤5, so max(helper(0,5), 4+helper(0,2)). helper(0,5): i=0, weight[0]=2 ≤5, so max(helper(-1,5), 3+helper(-1,3)). helper(-1,5)=0, helper(-1,3)=0, so helper(0,5)=max(0,3)=3. helper(0,2): i=0, weight[0]=2 ≤2, so max(helper(-1,2), 3+helper(-1,0)). helper(-1,2)=0, helper(-1,0)=0, so helper(0,2)=max(0,3)=3. So helper(1,5)=max(3, 4+3)=max(3,7)=7. helper(1,1): i=1, weight[1]=3 >1, so return helper(0,1). helper(0,1): i=0, weight[0]=2 >1, so return helper(-1,1)=0. So helper(2,5)=max(7, 5+0)=max(7,5)=7. Back to helper(3,10): max(12, 6+7)=max(12,13)=13. Final output: 13. Wait, check: With weights [2,3,4,5], values [3,4,5,6], capacity 10: Best is items 0,2,3 (weight 2+4+5=11 >10, invalid) or items 1,2,3 (weight 3+4+5=12 >10) or items 0,1,2 (weight 2+3+4=9, value 3+4+5=12) or items 0,1,3 (weight 2+3+5=10, value 3+4+6=13). Yes, 13 is correct. I wrote 10 earlier—correcting. Output is 13.

approaches.name: Approach 1: Brute Force Recursion O(2^n)
approaches.summary: Recursively explore all subsets of items by including or excluding each item, computing the maximum value without exceeding the knapsack capacity. This is straightforward but inefficient for large constraints due to exponential time.

ladders.title: Full Problem — 0/1 Knapsack with Brute Force Recursion
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution exploring all subsets to maximize value within capacity.
ladders.explanation: The 0/1 knapsack problem requires selecting items with given weights and values to maximize total value without exceeding a capacity, where each item can be taken at most once. The brute force recursion approach explores all possible subsets of items (2^n possibilities) by making a binary decision at each step: include the current item (if weight allows) or exclude it. This generates a complete decision tree where each leaf represents a valid subset. The intuition is that by recursively exploring both possibilities at each step, we guarantee checking every valid combination. The algorithm starts with the last item (index n-1) and works backward to the first, though forward traversal also works. At each recursive call with current index i and remaining capacity cap, we compute: 1) If i < 0 (base case), return 0 (no items left). 2) If weight[i] > cap, we cannot include item i, so return the result of excluding it (recursive call with i-1, same cap). 3) Otherwise, return the maximum of: a) Excluding item i: recursive call with i-1, cap. b) Including item i: value[i] + recursive call with i-1, cap - weight[i]. This ensures we consider both possibilities. Time complexity is O(2^n) because each call branches into two subproblems (include/exclude), creating an exponential tree. Space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack depth. Connection to L1: This full solution directly uses the recursive helper function from L1 as its core engine. Common mistakes include: forgetting base cases, incorrectly handling index bounds, or not considering both include/exclude cases properly. Real code pattern: def knapSack(cap, wt, val, n): def helper(i, cap): if i < 0: return 0 if wt[i] > cap: return helper(i-1, cap) return max(helper(i-1, cap), val[i] + helper(i-1, cap - wt[i])) return helper(n-1, cap).

examples.trace: Start: helper(2,7). i=2, weight[2]=5 ≤7, so max(helper(1,7), 60+helper(1,2)). Compute helper(1,7): i=1, weight[1]=4 ≤7, so max(helper(0,7), 50+helper(0,3)). Compute helper(0,7): i=0, weight[0]=3 ≤7, so max(helper(-1,7), 30+helper(-1,4)). helper(-1,7)=0 (base case). helper(-1,4)=0. So helper(0,7)=max(0,30+0)=30. Now compute helper(0,3): i=0, weight[0]=3 ≤3, so max(helper(-1,3), 30+helper(-1,0)). helper(-1,3)=0, helper(-1,0)=0, so helper(0,3)=max(0,30)=30. Back to helper(1,7): max(30, 50+30)=max(30,80)=80. Now compute helper(1,2): i=1, weight[1]=4 >2, so return helper(0,2). helper(0,2): i=0, weight[0]=3 >2, so return helper(-1,2)=0. Back to helper(2,7): max(80, 60+0)=max(80,60)=80. Final output: 80. Wait, correction: Let's recompute carefully. Actually, with weights [3,4,5] and values [30,50,60], capacity 7: Best is items 0 and 1 (weight 3+4=7, value 30+50=80). So output should be 80. My trace gave 80, but I wrote 90 earlier—correcting. Output is 80.

Start: helper(3,10). i=3, weight[3]=5 ≤10, so max(helper(2,10), 6+helper(2,5)). Compute helper(2,10): i=2, weight[2]=4 ≤10, so max(helper(1,10), 5+helper(1,6)). Compute helper(1,10): i=1, weight[1]=3 ≤10, so max(helper(0,10), 4+helper(0,7)). Compute helper(0,10): i=0, weight[0]=2 ≤10, so max(helper(-1,10), 3+helper(-1,8)). helper(-1,10)=0, helper(-1,8)=0, so helper(0,10)=max(0,3)=3. Compute helper(0,7): i=0, weight[0]=2 ≤7, so max(helper(-1,7), 3+helper(-1,5)). helper(-1,7)=0, helper(-1,5)=0, so helper(0,7)=max(0,3)=3. So helper(1,10)=max(3, 4+3)=max(3,7)=7. Compute helper(1,6): i=1, weight[1]=3 ≤6, so max(helper(0,6), 4+helper(0,3)). helper(0,6): i=0, weight[0]=2 ≤6, so max(helper(-1,6), 3+helper(-1,4)). helper(-1,6)=0, helper(-1,4)=0, so helper(0,6)=max(0,3)=3. helper(0,3): i=0, weight[0]=2 ≤3, so max(helper(-1,3), 3+helper(-1,1)). helper(-1,3)=0, helper(-1,1)=0, so helper(0,3)=max(0,3)=3. So helper(1,6)=max(3, 4+3)=max(3,7)=7. So helper(2,10)=max(7, 5+7)=max(7,12)=12. Now compute helper(2,5): i=2, weight[2]=4 ≤5, so max(helper(1,5), 5+helper(1,1)). helper(1,5): i=1, weight[1]=3 ≤5, so max(helper(0,5), 4+helper(0,2)). helper(0,5): i=0, weight[0]=2 ≤5, so max(helper(-1,5), 3+helper(-1,3)). helper(-1,5)=0, helper(-1,3)=0, so helper(0,5)=max(0,3)=3. helper(0,2): i=0, weight[0]=2 ≤2, so max(helper(-1,2), 3+helper(-1,0)). helper(-1,2)=0, helper(-1,0)=0, so helper(0,2)=max(0,3)=3. So helper(1,5)=max(3, 4+3)=max(3,7)=7. helper(1,1): i=1, weight[1]=3 >1, so return helper(0,1). helper(0,1): i=0, weight[0]=2 >1, so return helper(-1,1)=0. So helper(2,5)=max(7, 5+0)=max(7,5)=7. Back to helper(3,10): max(12, 6+7)=max(12,13)=13. Final output: 13. Wait, check: With weights [2,3,4,5], values [3,4,5,6], capacity 10: Best is items 0,2,3 (weight 2+4+5=11 >10, invalid) or items 1,2,3 (weight 3+4+5=12 >10) or items 0,1,2 (weight 2+3+4=9, value 3+4+5=12) or items 0,1,3 (weight 2+3+5=10, value 3+4+6=13). Yes, 13 is correct. I wrote 10 earlier—correcting. Output is 13.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Helper Function for Knapsack
ladders.desc: Recursive function that computes max value for items up to index i with given capacity.
ladders.explanation: This helper function is the heart of the brute force recursion approach. It solves a subproblem: given items from index 0 to i (inclusive) and a remaining capacity cap, what is the maximum achievable value? The intuition is to break the problem into smaller subproblems by considering the last item (index i). If we cannot include item i (due to weight), we must solve the subproblem for items 0 to i-1 with the same capacity. If we can include it, we have two choices: exclude it (same as previous case) or include it (add its value and solve for items 0 to i-1 with reduced capacity). By taking the maximum of these choices, we ensure optimality. The algorithm: function helper(i, cap): if i < 0: return 0 (base case, no items left). if weight[i] > cap: return helper(i-1, cap) (cannot include). else: return max(helper(i-1, cap), value[i] + helper(i-1, cap - weight[i])). Time complexity for this subproblem alone is O(2^i) in worst case, but when integrated into full solution, it contributes to overall O(2^n). Space is O(i) for recursion stack. Connection to L0: This function is called recursively in L0 to solve the entire problem by starting with i=n-1 and cap=full capacity. Common mistakes: incorrect base case (using i==0 instead of i<0), not checking weight before including, or misordering parameters. Real code pattern: def helper(i, cap): if i < 0: return 0 if wt[i] > cap: return helper(i-1, cap) return max(helper(i-1, cap), val[i] + helper(i-1, cap - wt[i])).

examples.trace: Call helper(2,7). i=2, weight[2]=5 ≤7, so compute max(helper(1,7), 60+helper(1,2)). First, helper(1,7): i=1, weight[1]=4 ≤7, so max(helper(0,7), 50+helper(0,3)). helper(0,7): i=0, weight[0]=3 ≤7, so max(helper(-1,7), 30+helper(-1,4)). helper(-1,7)=0, helper(-1,4)=0, so helper(0,7)=max(0,30)=30. helper(0,3): i=0, weight[0]=3 ≤3, so max(helper(-1,3), 30+helper(-1,0)). helper(-1,3)=0, helper(-1,0)=0, so helper(0,3)=max(0,30)=30. So helper(1,7)=max(30, 50+30)=max(30,80)=80. Now helper(1,2): i=1, weight[1]=4 >2, so return helper(0,2). helper(0,2): i=0, weight[0]=3 >2, so return helper(-1,2)=0. So max(80, 60+0)=max(80,60)=80. Output: 80.

Call helper(1,5). i=1, weight[1]=3 ≤5, so max(helper(0,5), 4+helper(0,2)). helper(0,5): i=0, weight[0]=2 ≤5, so max(helper(-1,5), 3+helper(-1,3)). helper(-1,5)=0, helper(-1,3)=0, so helper(0,5)=max(0,3)=3. helper(0,2): i=0, weight[0]=2 ≤2, so max(helper(-1,2), 3+helper(-1,0)). helper(-1,2)=0, helper(-1,0)=0, so helper(0,2)=max(0,3)=3. So max(3, 4+3)=max(3,7)=7. Output: 7.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Include/Exclude Decision at Each Item
ladders.desc: At each item, decide to include (if possible) or exclude to maximize value.
ladders.explanation: The core logic of the knapsack recursion is the binary decision made for each item: include it or exclude it. This decision is based on whether including the item improves the total value without exceeding capacity. The intuition is that by considering both possibilities independently, we explore all combinations. For item i with weight w and value v, and remaining capacity cap: If w > cap, we must exclude it. Otherwise, we compare two scenarios: 1) Exclude: ignore item i, so solution is whatever we get from items 0 to i-1 with same cap. 2) Include: take item i, add v to value, and solve for items 0 to i-1 with cap reduced by w. The maximum of these gives the optimal solution up to i. This works because the problem has optimal substructure—the optimal solution for i items either uses item i or doesn't. Step-by-step: Check if i < 0 (base case, return 0). Else, if weight[i] > cap, return result for i-1 items with same cap. Else, return max(result for i-1 items with same cap, value[i] + result for i-1 items with cap - weight[i]). Time for this decision is O(1), but it triggers recursive calls. Space is O(1) per call. Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the helper function's return statement. Common mistakes: including item when weight exceeds capacity, or not considering both options. Real code pattern: if wt[i] > cap: return helper(i-1, cap) else: return max(helper(i-1, cap), val[i] + helper(i-1, cap - wt[i])).

examples.input: item: weight=4, value=50, cap=7, prevBestExclude=30, prevBestIncludeReduced=20
examples.trace: Given: weight=4 ≤ cap=7, so we can include. Compute exclude option: prevBestExclude=30. Include option: value=50 + prevBestIncludeReduced=20 = 70. Take max(30,70)=70. So decision is to include, yielding value 70.

examples.input: item: weight=5, value=60, cap=3, prevBestExclude=40, prevBestIncludeReduced=0
examples.trace: Given: weight=5 > cap=3, so cannot include. Return prevBestExclude=40 directly. So decision is to exclude, value 40.

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive Subproblem Reduction
ladders.desc: Reducing knapsack problem to smaller subproblems by decreasing item index and capacity.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves breaking the knapsack problem into smaller instances by reducing the number of items considered and/or the remaining capacity. The intuition is that solving for i items and capacity cap can be expressed in terms of solutions for i-1 items (with same or reduced cap). This uses the principle of optimal substructure: an optimal solution for i items either uses the i-th item or doesn't. If it doesn't, the solution is same as for i-1 items with cap. If it does, the solution is value[i] plus solution for i-1 items with cap - weight[i]. By recursively applying this reduction, we eventually reach base cases (no items left). Step-by-step: To solve for i and cap, we need solutions for (i-1, cap) and (i-1, cap - weight[i]) if weight[i] ≤ cap. This creates a tree of subproblems. Time complexity arises from exponential number of subproblems. Space is linear in recursion depth. Connection to L2: This reduction is what enables the include/exclude decision—each decision reduces to a smaller subproblem. Common mistakes: not reducing index correctly (e.g., using i instead of i-1), or mishandling capacity reduction. Real code pattern: The recursive calls helper(i-1, cap) and helper(i-1, cap - wt[i]) embody this reduction.

examples.input: Reduce problem with i=2, cap=10 to subproblems, weights=[3,4,5], values=[30,50,60]
examples.trace: Given i=2, cap=10, weight[2]=5 ≤10. So we reduce to two subproblems: 1) Exclude item 2: solve for items 0-1 with cap=10, i.e., (i=1, cap=10). 2) Include item 2: solve for items 0-1 with cap reduced by 5, i.e., (i=1, cap=5). These are smaller problems with fewer items and/or less capacity.

examples.input: Reduce problem with i=0, cap=3 to subproblems, weights=[2], values=[5]
examples.trace: Given i=0, cap=3, weight[0]=2 ≤3. Reduce to: exclude: (i=-1, cap=3). include: (i=-1, cap=3-2=1). Both are base cases since i=-1.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Checking Weight Against Capacity
ladders.desc: Determine if an item can be included based on its weight and remaining capacity.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation is a simple comparison: check if the weight of the current item is less than or equal to the remaining capacity. If weight ≤ capacity, the item can be included; otherwise, it must be excluded. This is fundamental because inclusion is only allowed when there is enough space in the knapsack. The intuition is that we cannot exceed the capacity constraint, so this check acts as a gatekeeper. Step-by-step: Given item weight w and current capacity cap, perform the boolean check: w <= cap. If true, proceed to consider inclusion; if false, skip inclusion. Time and space are O(1). Connection to L3: This check determines whether we generate the include subproblem in the reduction—if weight > cap, we only generate the exclude subproblem. Common mistakes: using strict inequality (w < cap) which might exclude items with weight exactly equal to capacity, or misordering operands. Real code pattern: if wt[i] <= cap: ... else: ...

examples.trace: Compare 4 <= 7: true, so item can be included.
Compare 5 <= 3: false, so item cannot be included.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Recursion Base Case
ladders.desc: Define when recursion stops: no items left to consider.
ladders.explanation: The base case in recursion is the condition that stops further recursive calls, providing a direct answer. For the knapsack problem, the base case occurs when there are no items left to consider (i.e., index i is less than 0). At this point, the maximum value achievable is 0 because there are no items to select. This is intuitive: if you have an empty set of items, you cannot add any value, regardless of capacity. Step-by-step: In the recursive function, check if i < 0. If true, return 0. This prevents infinite recursion and provides a starting point for building up solutions. Time and space are O(1) for this check. Connection to L4: This base case is used after reducing the item index in subproblems—eventually, i becomes negative, and we return 0. Common mistakes: using i == 0 as base case (which would miss the case of no items), or forgetting the base case entirely. Real code pattern: if i < 0: return 0.

examples.trace: Since i=-1 < 0, we are at base case: no items left. Return 0.
i=-1 < 0, base case. Return 0, regardless of cap.

approaches.name: Approach 2: Dynamic Programming with 2D Array O(n*W)
approaches.summary: Use a 2D DP table where dp[i][w] stores the maximum value for the first i items with capacity w, iteratively filling it based on including or excluding items. This is optimal in time but uses significant space proportional to n*W.

ladders.title: Full Problem — 0/1 Knapsack with 2D DP Table
ladders.desc: Complete solution using 2D DP table to maximize value within capacity constraints.
ladders.explanation: The 0/1 knapsack problem requires selecting items with given weights and values to maximize total value without exceeding capacity W. The 2D DP approach solves this optimally by building a table dp[i][w] representing maximum value achievable using first i items with capacity w. Intuition: For each item i and capacity w, we have two choices - exclude item i (value = dp[i-1][w]) or include it if weight[i] ≤ w (value = value[i] + dp[i-1][w-weight[i]]). We take the maximum. This works because it explores all combinations systematically using optimal substructure - the solution for i items depends on solutions for i-1 items. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize dp[n+1][W+1] with zeros. 2) For i from 1 to n: For w from 0 to W: if weight[i-1] > w: dp[i][w] = dp[i-1][w]; else: dp[i][w] = max(dp[i-1][w], value[i-1] + dp[i-1][w-weight[i-1]]). 3) Return dp[n][W]. Time: O(n*W) from nested loops. Space: O(n*W) from 2D table. Connection: This is the complete implementation of Approach 2. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors in indices (0-indexed items vs 1-indexed DP table), incorrect initialization, not handling weight > w case properly. Real pattern: Build table bottom-up, use max of include/exclude decisions.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DP Table Filling for Single Item
ladders.desc: Process one item to update DP table row based on previous row.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine handles how to incorporate one item into the DP solution. Given previous row dp_prev representing solutions for i-1 items, and current item with weight wt and value val, compute new row dp_curr for i items. Intuition: For each capacity w from 0 to W, the maximum value either excludes the item (dp_prev[w]) or includes it if wt ≤ w (val + dp_prev[w-wt]). This captures the optimal decision for this item at each capacity. Why it works: It leverages optimal substructure - the best solution with i items builds upon best solutions with i-1 items. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize dp_curr array size W+1 with zeros. 2) For w from 0 to W: if wt > w: dp_curr[w] = dp_prev[w]; else: dp_curr[w] = max(dp_prev[w], val + dp_prev[w-wt]). 3) Return dp_curr. Time: O(W) per item. Space: O(W) for two rows. Connection: This is repeated for each item in L0 to build full table. Common mistakes: Not copying dp_prev when wt > w, incorrect index for dp_prev[w-wt], not iterating w from 0. Real pattern: Loop through capacities, apply include/exclude logic.

examples.trace: Initialize dp_curr[8]. w=0: wt=3>0, dp_curr[0]=dp_prev[0]=0. w=1: 3>1, dp_curr[1]=1. w=2: 3>2, dp_curr[2]=1. w=3: max(dp_prev[3]=1, 4+dp_prev[0]=4)=4. w=4: max(1,4+dp_prev[1]=5)=5. w=5: max(1,4+dp_prev[2]=5)=5. w=6: max(1,4+dp_prev[3]=5)=5. w=7: max(1,4+dp_prev[4]=5)=5.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Include/Exclude Decision for Item at Capacity
ladders.desc: Determine whether to include or exclude an item at specific capacity.
ladders.explanation: This core logic decides, for a given item with weight wt and value val, and given maximum value achievable without this item at capacities w and w-wt, whether to include or exclude the item to maximize value at capacity w. Intuition: At capacity w, we have two options: 1) Exclude item: value remains same as without item (prev[w]). 2) Include item: only possible if wt ≤ w, value becomes val + best value without item at remaining capacity (prev[w-wt]). The optimal choice is the maximum of these two. Why it works: It embodies the optimal substructure property - optimal solution either uses the item or doesn't. This local decision, when applied across all items and capacities, yields global optimum. Step-by-step: 1) Check if wt > w. If true, cannot include, return prev[w]. 2) Else, compute include_value = val + prev[w-wt]. 3) Return max(prev[w], include_value). Time: O(1) per decision. Space: O(1). Connection: This decision is made for each (item, capacity) pair in L1. Common mistakes: Forgetting to check wt ≤ w before including, using wrong index for prev[w-wt], not considering exclude case. Real pattern: Simple conditional with max.

examples.trace: wt=2 ≤ w=4, so include possible. include_value = 4 + prev[2] = 4+3=7. exclude_value = prev[4]=5. max(5,7)=7.
wt=5 > w=4, cannot include. Return exclude_value = prev[4]=6.

ladders.title: Building Block — Maximum of Two Values with Constraint Check
ladders.desc: Compute maximum of two values where one value has a prerequisite condition.
ladders.explanation: This building block implements a conditional maximum operation: given two potential values (exclude_val and include_val), return the larger, but include_val is only valid if a condition (weight ≤ capacity) is met. Intuition: In knapsack, including an item is only allowed if it fits in remaining capacity. This block encapsulates that logic cleanly. Why it's needed: It separates the decision logic from data access, making code modular. Step-by-step: 1) Check condition (wt ≤ w). 2) If false, return exclude_val. 3) If true, compute include_val = val + prev_remaining. 4) Return max(exclude_val, include_val). Time: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection: This is the essential operation inside L2's decision logic. Common mistakes: Evaluating include_val even when condition false (causing index error), not computing include_val correctly. Real pattern: if-else with max.

examples.input: exclude_val = 5, val = 3, prev_remaining = 2, condition = true (wt=2 ≤ w=3)
examples.trace: Condition true. include_val = 3 + 2 = 5. max(5,5)=5.

examples.input: exclude_val = 4, val = 6, prev_remaining = 1, condition = false (wt=5 > w=4)
examples.trace: Condition false. Return exclude_val=4.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Lookup and Addition
ladders.desc: Retrieve value from array and add to another number.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves accessing an element from an array using an index and adding it to a given value. Intuition: In knapsack DP, when including an item, we need the best value from previous solutions at reduced capacity (array lookup) and add current item's value. This is fundamental to building solutions incrementally. Why it's foundational: DP relies on combining previous results with current choices. Step-by-step: 1) Validate index is within array bounds (0 ≤ index < array length). 2) Retrieve array[index]. 3) Add given value to retrieved element. 4) Return sum. Time: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection: Used in L3 to compute include_val. Common mistakes: Index out of bounds (e.g., negative index when wt > w), not handling empty arrays. Real pattern: simple arithmetic with array access.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Basic array operations: creation, indexing, and element access.
ladders.explanation: Arrays are fundamental data structures that store elements in contiguous memory, accessible via indices. Intuition: In knapsack DP, we use arrays to store intermediate results (DP table). Understanding how to create arrays of specific sizes, access elements by index (0-based), and update values is prerequisite. Why it's essential: DP implementations heavily rely on arrays for tabulation. Step-by-step: 1) Creating array: In Python-style, use list comprehension [0]*(n+1) for size n+1. 2) Indexing: array[i] accesses element at position i (first element at index 0). 3) Updating: array[i] = value assigns new value. 4) Bounds: Ensure 0 ≤ i < len(array) to avoid errors. Time: O(1) for access/update. Space: O(n) for array. Connection: L4 uses array lookup. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors (e.g., using 1-based indexing when language uses 0-based), accessing out of bounds. Real pattern: declare array, use indices in loops.

examples.input: Create array of size 5 initialized to 0, set index 2 to 7, get value at index 2
examples.trace: array = [0, 0, 0, 0, 0]. array[2] = 7. Now array = [0, 0, 7, 0, 0]. array[2] returns 7.

examples.input: Create array [3, 1, 4], get value at index 1, then set index 0 to 5 and get value at index 0
examples.trace: array = [3, 1, 4]. array[1] is 1 (not output). array[0] = 5. Now array = [5, 1, 4]. array[0] returns 5.

testCases.input: Create array size 3 with all 1s, get index 0
Create array [5, 3, 9], set index 1 to 7, get index 1
Create array size 4 with zeros, set index 3 to 10, get index 3
Create array [1, 2, 3], get index 0, then set index 2 to 5, get index 2
Create array [0, 0, 0, 0], set index 0 to 8, get index 0
Create array size 2 with value 6, get index 1
Create array [4, 5, 6, 7], set index 3 to 1, get index 3

approaches.name: Approach 3: Dynamic Programming with 1D Array O(n*W)
approaches.summary: Optimize space by using a 1D DP array of size W+1, iterating items and updating capacities in reverse to avoid overwriting values from the same item. This reduces space usage while maintaining the same time complexity as the 2D approach.

ladders.title: Full Problem — 0/1 Knapsack with 1D DP Space Optimization
ladders.desc: Complete solution for 0/1 knapsack using 1D DP array with reverse iteration to optimize space.
ladders.explanation: The 0/1 knapsack problem requires selecting items with given weights and values to maximize total value without exceeding capacity W. The standard 2D DP approach uses O(n*W) space. We optimize to O(W) by observing that each row in the DP table depends only on the previous row. Using a 1D array dp[capacity] representing maximum value for each capacity, we process items one by one. The key insight is iterating capacities in reverse (from W down to weight[i]) to prevent overwriting values from the current item that would be needed for smaller capacities in the same iteration. This ensures each item is used at most once. Intuition: dp[c] stores the best value achievable with capacity c using items processed so far. Reverse iteration maintains the 'previous row' behavior of 2D DP. Step-by-step: Initialize dp array of size W+1 with zeros. For each item i from 0 to n-1: For capacity c from W down to weight[i]: dp[c] = max(dp[c], dp[c-weight[i]] + value[i]). After processing all items, dp[W] contains the answer. Time: O(n*W) from nested loops. Space: O(W) for the 1D array. Connection to L1: This implements the complete algorithm using the reverse iteration subroutine. Common mistakes: Forgetting reverse iteration (causes multiple uses of same item), incorrect array size (W+1), off-by-one errors in weight indexing. Real code pattern: def knapsack(values, weights, W): n = len(values); dp = [0]*(W+1); for i in range(n): for c in range(W, weights[i]-1, -1): dp[c] = max(dp[c], dp[c-weights[i]]+values[i]); return dp[W]

examples.trace: Initialize dp[0..4]=0. Item0 (v=1,w=4): c=4 to 4: dp[4]=max(0,dp[0]+1)=1. Item1 (v=2,w=5): skip (5>4). Item2 (v=3,w=1): c=4 to 1: dp[4]=max(1,dp[3]+3)=4, dp[3]=max(0,dp[2]+3)=3, dp[2]=3, dp[1]=3. Final dp[4]=4? Wait, check: After item2: dp[4]=max(1, dp[3]+3). dp[3] before update=0, so dp[4]=max(1,0+3)=3. Correct: dp[4]=3.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Reverse Capacity Iteration for 1D DP Update
ladders.desc: Implement the inner loop that updates DP array in reverse to handle 0/1 item constraint.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core mechanism enabling space optimization in 0/1 knapsack. Given a 1D DP array dp[0..W] representing best values for capacities, a current item with weight w and value v, we must update dp to include this item without using it multiple times. The intuition: In 2D DP, dp[i][c] uses dp[i-1][c-w] from the previous row. With 1D array, dp[c-w] must refer to the state before including current item. If we iterate capacities forward (c from w to W), dp[c-w] might have been updated in the same iteration (using current item), leading to multiple uses. Reverse iteration (c from W down to w) ensures dp[c-w] comes from the previous iteration (before current item), mimicking the 2D behavior. Step-by-step: For c = W down to w: newValue = dp[c-w] + v; if newValue > dp[c]: dp[c] = newValue. This updates each capacity only once per item. Time: O(W) per item. Space: O(W) for the array. Connection to L0: This subroutine is executed for each item in the full solution. Common mistakes: Using forward iteration, incorrect lower bound (should be w, not 0), not handling c<w cases. Real code pattern: for c in range(W, w-1, -1): dp[c] = max(dp[c], dp[c-w] + v). Example: dp=[0,0,0,0], w=2,v=5,W=3. Reverse: c=3: dp[3]=max(0,dp[1]+5)=5; c=2: dp[2]=max(0,dp[0]+5)=5. Forward would give dp[2]=5, then c=3 uses updated dp[1]=0 (okay here but fails in general).

ladders.title: Core Logic — State Transition in 0/1 Knapsack DP
ladders.desc: Understand the DP recurrence relation: dp[c] = max(exclude item, include item).
ladders.explanation: The core logic of 0/1 knapsack is the state transition that decides whether to include an item at a given capacity. For each item i with weight w and value v, and capacity c, we have two choices: exclude the item (value remains dp[c] from previous state) or include it (value becomes dp[c-w] + v, where dp[c-w] is best value with remaining capacity). The DP recurrence is: dp[c] = max(dp[c], dp[c-w] + v) if w <= c, else dp[c] remains. Intuition: This maximizes total value by considering all combinations efficiently via DP. It works because problems have optimal substructure—the best solution for capacity c either uses item i or doesn't. Step-by-step: Initialize dp[c]=0 for all c. For each item, apply the recurrence for c where w<=c. Time: O(1) per state update. Space: O(W) for storing states. Connection to L1: This recurrence is applied during reverse iteration in the subroutine. Common mistakes: Forgetting the exclude case, incorrect indexing for dp[c-w], not handling c<w. Real code pattern: if c >= w: dp[c] = max(dp[c], dp[c-w] + v). Example: At capacity 5 with item w=2,v=7, dp[5] = max(previous dp[5], dp[3]+7). This captures whether adding the item improves value.

examples.trace: Include value: dp[3]+7=4+7=11. Exclude value: 10. max(10,11)=11. So new dp[5]=11.
Include: dp[1]+5=2+5=7, but w=3>c=4? Wait, c=4, w=3, so include is valid: dp[4-3]=dp[1]=2, 2+5=7. Exclude: 6. max(6,7)=7. Output should be 7. Correction: dp[4]=max(6, dp[1]+5)=max(6,2+5)=7.

ladders.title: Building Block — 1D Array for DP State Storage
ladders.desc: Use a 1D array to store intermediate results for knapsack capacities.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves creating and manipulating a 1D array to store the maximum achievable value for each knapsack capacity. In DP, we need to remember solutions to subproblems—here, dp[c] represents the best value possible with capacity c using items processed so far. The intuition: Instead of a 2D table (items x capacities), we collapse the item dimension because each row depends only on the previous row. A 1D array of size W+1 suffices, initialized to zeros (no items, value 0). As we process items, we update this array in-place. Key points: Index c corresponds to capacity, dp[c] is the value, array length is W+1 to include capacity 0. Step-by-step: Create array dp with W+1 zeros. For each item, update dp using the recurrence. Time: O(W) to initialize. Space: O(W). Connection to L2: This array stores the states where the recurrence is applied. Common mistakes: Using size W instead of W+1, not initializing to zero, incorrect indexing. Real code pattern: dp = [0] * (W + 1). Example: W=5, dp=[0,0,0,0,0,0]. After processing some items, dp might become [0,3,6,6,9,9].

examples.trace: Create array of size W+1=4. Initialize all elements to 0: dp=[0,0,0,0].

examples.output: Invalid: size mismatch, but if we set dp=[5,10,15] for W=2, it has size 3, which is W+1=3, so okay. Trace: dp[0]=5, dp[1]=10, dp[2]=15.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Over Array in Reverse
ladders.desc: Loop through an array from end to start, updating elements based on earlier indices.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves traversing an array in decreasing index order, which is crucial for the 1D knapsack update to prevent item reuse. The intuition: When updating dp[c] using dp[c-w], if we go forward (c increasing), dp[c-w] may have been updated in the same pass, corrupting the 0/1 constraint. Reverse iteration ensures dp[c-w] refers to the state before current updates. Step-by-step: For an array arr of length n, iterate i from n-1 down to 0 (or a specific start). At each i, you can access arr[i] and arr[i-k] for some k. Time: O(n) for the loop. Space: O(1) extra. Connection to L3: This operation is used to update the 1D DP array. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors in loop bounds, incorrect step size, modifying array while iterating. Real code pattern: for i in range(start, end-1, -1): # process arr[i]. Example: arr=[1,2,3,4], iterate from index 3 to 0: i=3: arr[3]=4; i=2: arr[2]=3; etc.

examples.trace: Loop i=3: access arr[3]=4. i=2: arr[2]=3. i=1: arr[1]=2. i=0: arr[0]=1.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Understand basic array operations: creation, access, and modification by index.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept covers arrays, which are contiguous data structures storing elements accessible by index. In programming, an array allows O(1) access to any element using its position. For knapsack DP, we use an array to store values for each capacity. Intuition: Think of an array as a list of boxes numbered from 0 to n-1, each holding a value. You can create an array of a specific size, set all elements to an initial value (like 0), read a value at an index, and update it. Step-by-step: To create an array of size n: arr = [initialValue] * n. To access element at index i: val = arr[i]. To modify: arr[i] = newValue. Time: O(1) for access/update. Space: O(n) for storage. Connection to L4: Reverse iteration builds on accessing array elements by index. Common mistakes: Index out of bounds (using index >= n), confusing 0-based indexing, not initializing properly. Real code pattern: arr = [0] * 5; arr[2] = 10; x = arr[2] # x becomes 10. Example: Array of size 3 for capacities 0,1,2: dp = [0,0,0]; dp[1] = 5; now dp is [0,5,0].

examples.input: Create array size 4 with initial value 0, set index 2 to 7
Array [1,2,3], read index 1, update index 0 to 5
examples.trace: Start arr=[1,2,3]. Read arr[1]=2. Update arr[0]=5. New arr=[5,2,3].
